text:
Admission Date:  2016-06-17     Discharge Date:  1974-08-07

Date of Birth:   1983-01-17     Sex:  M

Service:  CARDIOTHORACIC

CHIEF COMPLAINT:  Chest pain and shortness of breath.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:  The patient had onset of angina
in 2016 and cardiac catheterization at that time revealed
left circumflex disease which was angioplastied and stented.
He returned to the catheterization laboratory in 1980-03-17
for recurrent angina.  This revealed ISR which was
angioplastied and an RCA stent was placed.  He returned the
next day for recurrent angina and all treatment sites were
patent.  In 1975-07-03, he was admitted to a local
hospital with recurrent angina and severe anemia.  He ruled
out for a myocardial infarction at that time.  In 1985-12-20, he was brought to the catheterization laboratory after
a positive exercise tolerance test.  The catheterization
revealed a 50% PDA lesion, 20% left main, 50% LAD, 50% ostial
left circumflex, ejection fraction of 60%, and FFR of the RCA
of 0.88.  He returned in 15 for an RCA stent.  In
03 he was admitted with unstable angina and ruled out for
myocardial infarction.  He was brought to the catheterization
laboratory again in 28 for recurrent angina and a positive
exercise tolerance test which revealed 40-50% PDA, 30% left
main, 60-70% LAD, 50% circumflex, and 60% OM lesions.  An FFR
of the left circumflex was 0.91 and the LAD was 0.73.  The
LAD was stented, jailing the diagonal.  The procedure was
complicated by a retroperitoneal hematoma.  The patient had
done well until this week when he noted return of exertional
angina.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:  The patient's past medical history is
significant for anemia, gastroesophageal reflux disease, mild
aortic stenosis, and coronary artery disease with multiple
interventional procedures.

ALLERGIES:  The patient is allergic to Nitroglycerin which
causes severe hypertension and IVP dye which causes nausea
and vomiting.

MEDICATIONS PRIOR TO ADMISSION:  Cardizem CD 240 mg q.d.,
aspirin 325 mg q.d., Prevacid 30 mg q.d., Lipitor 10 mg q.d.,
Atenolol 75 mg q.p.m., ferrous sulfate 325 mg q.d.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION ON ADMISSION:  Vital signs revealed
heart rate 63, blood pressure 124/66, respiratory rate 18, O2
saturation 94%.  The neck was supple with no jugular venous
distention.  The carotids were 1+ with no bruits.
Cardiovascular examination revealed regular rate and rhythm,
S1 and S2, 3/6 systolic ejection murmur in the upper sternal
border and the left sternal border.  The chest was clear to
auscultation bilaterally.  The abdomen was soft, nontender,
and nondistended.  On extremity examination, femoral pulses
were 2+ bilaterally with no bruit, dorsalis pedis 1+
bilaterally, posterior tibialis 1+ bilaterally, no edema.

LABORATORY DATA:  Electrocardiogram revealed sinus rhythm
with ST depressions in I, II, aVL, and V2-V6, T wave
inversions in III and aVF, and ST elevations in III.

HOSPITAL COURSE:  The patient was admitted to the
catheterization laboratory where he underwent cardiac
catheterization.  Please see the catheterization report for
full details.  Summary of the catheterization showed the LAD
with 80-90% in-stent, circumflex 70%, OM-1 60%, RCA 50%
in-stent, right PDA 60%.  The patient was referred to
Cardiothoracic Surgery for evaluation of coronary artery
bypass grafting as well as aortic valve replacement.  The
patient was accepted onto the Cardiothoracic Service and on
2011-02-20, he was brought to the Operating Room
where he underwent coronary artery bypass grafting times
three with a LIMA to the LAD and saphenous vein grafts to the
OM and RCA.  He also underwent an aortic valve replacement
with a #21 pericardial valve.  Please see the operative
report for full details.  The patient tolerated the operation
well and was transferred from the Operating Room to the
Cardiothoracic Intensive Care Unit.  At the time of transfer,
his mean arterial pressure was 75, CVP was 8, PA was 38/15,
he was A paced at 90 beats per minute, and he was on Propofol
infusion.  He also had arterial and Swan-Ganz catheters, two
ventricular and two atrial pacing wires, and two mediastinal
and left pleural chest tubes.  The patient did well in the
immediate postoperative period.  He was weaned from the
ventilator and extubated on the day of his surgery.  On the
morning of postoperative day #1, he was scheduled to be
transferred to the step-down floor for continuing
postoperative care and cardiac rehabilitation.  However there
were no beds available at that time and he remained in the
Intensive Care Unit unit.  On postoperative day #2, the
patient had an episode of rapid atrial fibrillation with a
ventricular response between 130 and 160 beats per minute.
He tolerated this hemodynamically.  He was loaded with
Amiodarone and treated with intravenous Lopressor for rate
control.  After a short time in atrial fibrillation, the
patient converted to normal sinus rhythm.  Over the next
several days, the patient had an uneventful postoperative
course.  His activity level was increased on a daily basis
with the assistance of physical therapy.  Hemodynamically he
remained stable.  On postoperative day #6, he was deemed to
be stable and read for discharge to home with the VNA.  At
the time of discharge, the patient's condition was stable and
his physical examination was as follows.  Vital signs
revealed temperature 97.1, heart rate 75 and sinus rhythm,
blood pressure 118/58, respiratory rate 18, O2 saturation 96%
on room air.  His weight preoperatively was 80.2 kg and at
discharge was 76.9 kg.  Laboratory data revealed white blood
cell count 9, hematocrit 29, platelets 188,00, sodium 140,
potassium 4.1, chloride 105, CO2 25, BUN 29, creatinine 1.2,
glucose 104.  On physical examination, the patient was alert
and oriented times three, moved all extremities, and followed
commands.  Respiratory examination revealed breath sounds
clear to auscultation bilaterally.  Heart sounds revealed
regular rate and rhythm, S1 and S2, and no murmurs, rubs, or
gallops.  The sternum was stable and the incision was closed
with Steri-Strips, open to air, and clean and dry.  The
abdomen was soft, nontender, and nondistended with
normoactive bowel sounds.  The extremities were warm and well
perfused.  There was no clubbing, cyanosis, or edema.  The
right leg incision was with Steri-Strips, open to air, and
clean and dry.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:  Lipitor 10 mg q.d., Lopressor 50 mg
b.i.d., Captopril 150 mg t.i.d., aspirin 81 mg q.d.,
Amiodarone 400 mg b.i.d. x 7 days the q.d. thereafter x 1
month.  The patient's p.r.n. medications include ibuprofen
400-600 mg q. 6 hours p.r.n. and Percocet 5/325 mg 1-2
tablets p.o. q. 4 hours p.r.n.

DISCHARGE STATUS:  The patient is to be discharged home with
VNA.

DISCHARGE FOLLOWUP:  The patient's followup is to be in the
Mission Hospital Laguna Beach Clinic in two weeks and with Dr. Sims in four
weeks as well as with his primary care physician in three to
four weeks.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSES:  Coronary artery disease, status post
coronary artery bypass grafting times three; aortic stenosis,
status post aortic valve replacement; hypertension;
hypercholesterolemia; anemia; gastroesophageal reflux
disease.






                          [**First Name11 (Name Pattern1) **] [**Initials (NamePattern4) **] Baker, M.D.  58738
